segments.s116-g33: خروشیدن is the verbal noun (masdar) ‘roaring / to roar’ used as the event that acts like the subject; آمد is a past-tense 3rd-person singular verb ‘came / arrived’ placed after the verbal noun (a poetic word order). به گوش is a prepositional phrase ‘to the ear / was heard’ indicating the event reached someone's hearing. Together the clause means ‘the roaring came to the ear / was heard’.
segments.s116-g34: روان و دلم is a coordinated subject ‘my soul and my heart’ (دلم written here with a separate m particle in the text); تازه شد is a past-tense copular construction ‘became fresh / was renewed’ where شد is ‘became’. زان خروش is a prepositional/agent-like phrase ‘from that roar / because of that cry’ (زان = ‘from that’). The whole clause says the soul and heart were renewed by that roar.
segments.s116-g35: به گاه is a frozen prepositional expression meaning ‘at the time of / in the days of’; قباد is the proper name (Qobad). این خروشش is ‘this roar/this outcry (of his)’ with the possessive sense folded into the phrase; نکرد is the negated past-tense verb ‘did not do / did not make’. The clause means ‘in Qobad’s time he did not make this outcry.’
segments.s116-g36: کجا کرد is a short interrogative/rhetorical phrase literally ‘where did (he) do/make’; with کرد as past ‘did’ it asks/location or rhetorical ‘where/when did he (ever) do such a thing’. با شاه ترکان نبرد is a prepositional phrase ‘against/with the Turkish king, a battle’ (با = ‘with/against’, شاه ترکان = ‘the Turkish king’, نبرد = ‘battle’). Together: ‘Where did he engage in battle with the Turkish king?’
segments.s116-g37: بیامد is past 3rd-person singular ‘came’; هم adds ‘also / too’ and is placed after the verb for emphasis (poetic word order). اندر زمان is the temporal phrase ‘in that time / at that moment’; پیش اوی uses پیش ‘before / in the presence of’ and اوی (archaic form) ‘him’ — so the phrase means ‘he also came before him at that time.’
segments.s116-g38: This segment is a string of epithets modifying the newcomer: یل ‘hero’ (noun) followed by compound/adjectival epithets دانش‌افروز ‘knowledge-illuminating / enlightening’ and پرخاشجوی ‘aggressive / quarrelsome’. In Persian poetry such modifiers typically follow or cluster with the noun to create a compact description.
segments.s116-g39: به نزدیک کاووس is a locative phrase ‘near to Kavus’ (به + نزدیک + proper name). شد is the past-tense copula/verb ‘became / came to be’ used here to mean ‘arrived / approached’. پیلتن is a place name (or group name) and is the subject that ‘became/arrived’ near Kavus: ‘Piltan drew near to Kavus.’
segments.s116-g40: همه سرفرازان is the subject ‘all the proud/victorious ones’; شدند is the past plural of شدن ‘became / were’ and here functions as ‘they assembled / were gathered’; انجمن is the predicative noun ‘assembly / council’. The clause therefore means ‘all the nobles/warriors gathered in council.’
segments.s116-g25: The clause is S (subject) + prepositional phrase + verb + object: تهمتن (subject) + ز اولاد (ز = poetic 'from/among') indicates source/whom he asked, پرسید is the past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'asked', and راه is the direct object 'the road/way'. Word order places the source phrase immediately after the subject (common in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s116-g26: This is a relative/interrogative-location clause describing the destination: به شهری (به + شهری = 'to/into a city') followed by the interrogative کجا 'where', then the past copula بود 'was', and finally the person-location identifier کاووس شاه. Classical word order places the locative phrase and question-word before بود and the named person after it.
segments.s116-g27: چو بشنید ازو is a temporal clause: چو 'when', بشنید past 3rd-sing 'he heard', ازو 'from him/about him'. تیز is an adverb 'swiftly/keenly' modifying the verb; بنهاد is the archaic past of نهاد 'to put/set' used idiomatically here with روی 'face' (بنهاد روی = 'he set/turned his face' i.e. he faced/started off). The phrase order places the adverb before the verb phrase, typical in poetry.
segments.s116-g28: پیاده دوان is a compound verbal phrase: پیاده 'on foot' qualifies the manner and dوان (present/past-stem form) 'running, rushing' gives the action. پیش او 'before/in front of him' indicates direction/toward him, and راهجوی is the agent noun 'the way-seeker/wayfarer' (here the runner). In this poetic ordering the manner (on foot, running) comes first, then the directional phrase, and finally the noun identifying who performed it.
segments.s116-g29: چو آمد is a temporal 'when he came/entered' (چو = when, آمد past 'came'). به شهر اندرون = 'into the inner part of the city' (به + شهر + اندرون), and تاجبخش follows as the subject performing the next action; poetic inversion places the proper name after the locative phrase.
segments.s116-g30: خروشی برآورد is a verb phrase where خروشی 'a shout/roar' is the thing produced and برآورد (past) means 'brought forth/raised' (an archaic/pet poetic use of برآورد = 'let out'). چون introduces a simile 'like', رعد رخش literally 'the thunder of Rakhsh' (Rakhsh being the famous steed) — the phrase means his cry resembled the horse's thunderous sound.
segments.s116-g31: به ایرانیان گفت is 'he said to the Iranians' (به + indirect object phrase before گفت), with پس 'then' marking sequence. شهریار immediately after functions as a vocative/interjection meaning 'O king/sovereign' addressing the ruler in the reported speech; in poetry the vocative can appear inside the reporting clause.
segments.s116-g32: که introduces the content clause 'that': که بر ما سرآمد بد روزگار = 'that upon us has come bad days/fate'. بر ما 'upon us', سرآمد here is used idiomatically as 'has befallen/come upon' (older/poetic use), and بد روزگار 'ill time/evil fate' is the object of that verb phrase.
segments.s116-g1: یکی is the indefinite 'a/certain'; مغفری is an archaic noun here (mughfarī) describing a royal object/person related to pardon or a pardoning cap — a nominal phrase modified by خسروی 'royal/kingly'. بر سرش is a prepositional phrase 'on his head' (بر + سر + enclitic ش 'his'), so the whole segment says 'a certain royal maghfarī on his head'.
segments.s116-g2: خوی آلوده is the object phrase 'tainted habit/disposition' (خوی + آلوده). ببر is an imperative from بردن 'take!' addressed to someone, so the structure is verb (imperative) after its object; در برش is an adverbial/locative phrase 'into/within it' (در + برش where ش = 'his/it'), and بیان here is an awkward/archaic connector (likely emphatic or colloquial) placed before the locative. The sequence thus reads roughly 'Take his tainted habit and put it into it.'
segments.s116-g3: به + proper name + title (ارژنگ سالار) marks direction/target 'toward Arzhang the commander'. بنهاد is the past of بنهادن 'to place/lay' used idiomatically with روی 'face' to mean 'he turned/laid his face toward' (i.e., 'he looked toward'). Word order is typical older Persian: preposition + object, then verb + complement.
segments.s116-g4: چو آمد = 'when he came' (چو = when); بر لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'upon/to the army/host' (بر + لشکر); نامجوی is a descriptive compound 'seeking a name' (fame‑seeking) modifying the host or indicating its character. The clause gives the temporal clause for the previous action.
segments.s116-g5: یکی is indefinite 'someone'; نعره زد is a compound past 'shouted/cried out' (نعره = shout + زد = struck/uttered); در میان گروه is the locative phrase 'in the midst of the group/among the ranks'. The verb زد is used with نعره to mean 'let out a shout'.
segments.s116-g6: تو گفتی is direct/reported speech 'you said' (classical second‑person past form); بدرید here is the quoted verb 'to tear (apart)' — in reported speech it appears in the simple form, representing the action promised or boasting about (i.e., 'you said: "tear up sea and mountain"'). دریا و کوه are the coordinated objects 'sea and mountain.'
segments.s116-g7: برون آمد = 'came out/stepped forth' (compound adverb + past verb). از خیمه = 'from the tent' (از + خیمه). ارژنگ دیو places the proper name and the appositive title 'dīv' (demon/ogre) together: 'Arzhang the demon.' The sequence is straightforward narrative past.
segments.s116-g8: چو آمد = 'when it/that came' (temporal 'when'); به گوش اندرش = 'to his ear' where به گوش = 'to the ear' and اندر + ش = 'in/within him/his' (archaic locative + enclitic); آن غریو = 'that roar/clamor.' Word order places the temporal clause after the main clause, common in narrative poetry.
segments.s116-g41: غریوید is a past narrative verb (third person plural) meaning “they cried out/roared”; بسیار is an adverb 'very/much' modifying it. و simply links the clauses. بردش is written with the enclitic -ش (him) but its lexical senses given do not fit the context (here it functions as a verb meaning 'they made/held for him' or 'they offered for him'); نماز is the object 'prayer' and is the thing offered/held. Word order is typical older/poetic Persian: verb then object phrases joined by و.
segments.s116-g42: بپرسیدش is a past narrative verb with the object enclitic -ش 'him' meaning 'they asked him.' از introduces the topic/concern ('about' / 'concerning'), followed by رنجهای 'the sufferings' in the plural and دراز 'long' modifying رنجها. The phrase literally 'asked him about the long sufferings.'
segments.s116-g43: گرفتش is a past verb 'took/took hold of him' with the enclitic object -ش 'him.' به آغوش is a prepositional phrase 'into the bosom/embrace of,' followed by کاووس شاه 'King Kavus' (title after the name is a normal poetic order). The whole phrase means 'they placed/took him into King Kavus's arms.'
segments.s116-g44: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from/of' used here to mark the source of the question: 'asked of Zal' (i.e. 'asked Zal'). زالش is the name زال with the enclitic -ش; بپرسید is the past narrative 'he/they asked.' و links to the next phrase; از introduces the topic 'concerning' or 'about' and رنج راه 'the hardships of the road' (راه = 'road, way').
segments.s116-g45: بدو گفت is the common older construction 'he said to him' where بدو = 'to him' and گفت is the past 'said.' پنهان functions as a predicate/adjective 'hidden.' ازین is the fused preposition + demonstrative 'from these' (از + این), and جادوان is plural 'the enchanters/sorcerers.' Together: 'He told him: (let him be) hidden from these sorcerers.'
segments.s116-g46: همی is an archaic/poetic prefatory particle that gives an iterative or continuative flavor (often 'now/so/then' or marking the narrative action). رخش 'Rakhsh' is the steed; را is the definite object marker. کرد is the past verb 'made/did' (here causative: 'made/caused'), and باید روان is a fused predicate 'must be set moving / must be made to go' (باید = 'must/it is necessary', روان used adjectivally/adverbially 'in motion/flowing' = 'ready to go'). Grammatically this is: [particle] + object + verb + predicate 'must be...'.
segments.s116-g47: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' آید is the narrative/presentive verb 'comes' (here used as 'reaches' or 'arrives at'). به + دیو سپید is the prepositional phrase 'to the White Demon' (دیو modified by سپید). آگهی means 'news' or 'information' — so: 'when the news reached the White Demon.'
segments.s116-g48: کز is the relative/connector 'that/which' (classical poetic form of که + ز); ارژنگ is the personal name 'Arzhang' as subject. شد is the past verb 'became' and introduces a result/clause. روی گیتی is a genitival phrase 'the face of the world' (روی = face/surface, گیتی = world). تهی is the predicate adjective 'empty' — 'that Arzhang caused the face of the world to become empty.'
segments.s116-g73: چکانی here is a verbal/adverbial form built from چکان(دن) meaning 'to drip' and functions like a conditional/occurrent 'if/when it drips'. سه قطره is the quantity phrase 'three drops' (noun after numeral), and به چشم اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'into the eye, inward' where به + noun = 'into/into the interior of' and اندرون reinforces 'inside'.
segments.s116-g74: شود is the classical/past-oriented present-subjunctive/passive 'becomes' or 'will be'; تیرگی is the subject 'darkness/obscuration' and پاک is a predicate adjective (here 'cleared, clean' or 'wholly removed'). با خون برون is an instrumental/ablative phrase 'with/by blood, outward' where برون = 'out/forth'; altogether: 'the darkness becomes cleared and blood is expelled.'
segments.s116-g75: گو here heads the name phrase (Go‑/Gupiltan) rather than functioning as the verb 'say' — in classical texts such particles often appear in names; جنگ را ساز کرد is an idiom where ساز (preparation/means) + کرد ('made/did') means 'prepared for war', and را marks جنگ as the definite/accusative object.
segments.s116-g76: ازان جایگه = 'from that place' (ازان = 'from that'); رفتن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to go' used as the object of آغاز; آغاز کرد is the compound 'began' (آغاز = 'beginning' + کرد = 'did/made'), so رفتن آغاز کرد = 'he began to go' or 'he set out.'
segments.s116-g77: به ایرانیان گفت = 'he said to the Iranians' (به marks the indirect object). بیدار بید is an exhortation: بیدار = 'awake/alert' (adjective) and بید is an archaic jussive/imperative copular form meaning 'be' (plural/command), so together 'Be awake!'.
segments.s116-g78: که introduces the reported content ('that/for'); من کردم is simple past 'I made/did' (کردم = 'I did'), and آهنگ دیو سپید is the object: آهنگ here means 'intention/aim/plan' (i.e., an attack or design) and دیو سپید = 'the White Demon'. The phrase means 'that I have set my intention/aim against the White Demon.'
segments.s116-g79: یکی 'one/a certain' introduces the subject; پیل جنگی = 'a war‑elephant' where جنگی is a post‑nominal adjective 'warlike/for war'. و چاره‌گرست is a copular predicate: چاره‌گر ('one who finds a remedy' or 'resource') + ‑ست (archaic 'is'), so 'and it is a remedy/resource.'
segments.s116-g80: فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'plentiful(ly)'; به گرداندرش is a genitival/prepositional phrase 'in his retinue' (گرداندرش = 'in his company/retinue'), and لشکرست uses لشکر ('army') + ‑ست ('is') to state the predicate 'there is an army.' Word order is inverted for poetic meter: 'plentiful, in his retinue is an army.'
segments.s116-g81: "گر" is the conditional 'if'; "ایدونک" is an archaic/poetic particle here functioning as 'now' or an emphatic temporal marker. The verb "آرد" (from آوردن) is a classical 3rd-person form used here in a hypothetical sense 'is/should be brought' so the clause means 'if now my back is brought to a bend' (i.e., if my back is bent/overwhelmed). Word order is typical older Persian: subject "پشت من" ('my back') precedes the verb phrase and the preposition "به" + noun "خم" marks the result 'into a bend.'
segments.s116-g82: This is a jussive/optative clause expressing a wish or curse: "شما" (you, plural) + adverb "دیر" ('for a long time') + verb "مانید" (present-subjunctive/imperative plural 'may you remain'). "خوار و دژم" are predicative adjectives ('despised and sullen') that describe the state in which 'you' are to remain. The sequence puts the duration adverb before the verb ("دیر مانید") and the adjectives follow as complements.
segments.s116-g83: "وگر" = 'but if' introduces a conditional; "یار باشد" uses "باشد" in the present-subjunctive sense 'there be' (classical Persian conditional 'if there be a friend/ally'). "خداوند هور" is the subject phrase that will govern the verb in the next segment (literal 'the Lord (God) [may give] splendor/light'); the verb is deferred to the following segment, a normal poetic inversion.
segments.s116-g84: The verb "دهد" is 3rd-person singular present used optatively/hortatively here ('may he give' / 'may he grant'). The pronoun cluster "مر مرا ... را" functions as the recipient/object marking in poetic/clitic form (here conveying 'to me'); "اختر نیک زور" is the object phrase 'an auspicious/fortunate star of strength' ("نیک" qualifying the star, "زور" = 'strength/might'). The clause therefore reads as a wish: 'may (the Lord) give me an auspicious, powerful star.'
segments.s116-g85: همان بوم و بر is a fixed, nominal phrase meaning “that same homeland/place” (همان = “the same/that” modifying the compound بوم و بر). باز یابید is a second-person plural imperative/subjunctive form of یابیدن (یابید), with باز (“again”) placed immediately before the verb to mean “find/recapture again.” The final و تخت attaches by coordination: تخت (“throne”) is another direct object coordinated with بوم و بر, so the whole segment commands “(you) regain that same homeland and the throne.”
segments.s116-g86: به بار آید is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning “to come to bear (fruit)/to come into bearing,” with به + بار forming the notion of a ‘load/fruit’ and آید (3rd person singular of آمدن) as the verbal core. Poetic inversion places the verb phrase before its subject: آن خسروانی درخت = “that royal tree” follows آید and is the logical subject; آن is a demonstrative determiner (“that”), خسروانی an adjectival/attributive form (“royal”), and درخت the noun (“tree”). Together: “and that royal tree will come to bear (fruit).”
segments.s116-g17: نکردند is the 3rd-person plural past negative of کردن ('they did not do'); here it combines with the noun یاد to form the idiom یاد کردن = 'to remember' (so 'they did not remember'). بر بوم is a prepositional phrase ('on/upon the homeland') with بر as the preposition and بوم 'homeland'. The final رست is a short, poetic past verb (from رستن) meaning 'escaped/fled' and is coordinated by و ('and').
segments.s116-g18: This clause lists the actors and the action: پدر بر پسر is a poetic coordination ('father and son'—the repeated preposition بر is a rhetorical device here), همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'just/straight/at once', and راه جست is the verbal phrase 'took/leapt onto the road' (جست, past of جستن, used here in the sense 'set out'). Word order places the coordinated subjects before the verb.
segments.s116-g19: برآهیخت is a past, intransitive verb 'rose up/was roused' placed before its nominal phrase for poetic emphasis. شمشیر کین پیلتن is a genitive/grouping: شمشیر ('sword') modified by کین ('vengeance, hatred') and then by the proper name پیلتن ('of Piltan') — i.e. 'Piltan's sword of vengeance.'
segments.s116-g20: بپردخت is a past tense verb (3rd sing.) from پرداختن here meaning 'he struck/dealt (a blow) / he made a move'; یکباره is an adverb 'suddenly/at once'; زان is a fused prepositional particle 'from that' and انجمن is the noun 'assembly/meeting' object of زان. The sequence gives 'he struck suddenly from that assembly.'
segments.s116-g21: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. برگشت is the past verb 'returned' and is followed by the epithets پیروز ('victorious') and گیتی‌فروز ('world‑illuminating'), which function as his titles/appositives — a common poetic ordering where چو + verb introduces the temporal clause before naming the subject.
segments.s116-g22: بیامد is the past 'he came'; دمان here means 'the foot/foothill (edge of the hill)'; تا + به is a spatial expression 'as far as / up to'; کوه اسپروز is the destination 'Mount Asporoz' (کوه + proper name). The preposition به precedes کوه اسپروز to mark the place reached.
segments.s116-g23: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/among'. اولاد is 'offspring/children' (the group from which an action is taken). بگشاد is a past form meaning 'opened/loosed' (here used of making the loop), and خم کمند is the object 'the bend/loop of the lasso' (خم modifying کمند). Together: 'from the offspring they opened (or cast) the lasso's loop.'
segments.s116-g24: نشستند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they sat down' (classical نشستن); زیر is the preposition 'under/beneath'; درختی is an indefinite/tree noun 'a tree'; بلند is the adjective 'tall'. The phrase means 'they sat beneath a tall tree.'
segments.s116-g57: گذر کرد باید is a poetic word order for the modal construction باید + verb: باید گذر کرد = 'one must pass'. گذر is a noun meaning 'passage' and here functions as the verbal noun; کرد is the past of کردن used with the noun to make the verbal expression. بر هفت کوه places the action 'over seven mountains' after the verb; بر here means 'over/on' (i.e. across).
segments.s116-g58: This clause is descriptive: ز + noun indicates origin/agent ('from' / 'among'), followed by a goal phrase به هر جای 'in/at every place'. کرده is the perfect/past participle or past verb 'made/formed' and گروه is the noun 'group/troop' (here a troop of divs). Poetic word order places the verb before its logical subject/agent.
segments.s116-g59: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (not just the numeral), غار 'cave'. پیش آیدت is the verb phrase آید + enclitic pronoun ت = 'it/ he comes/befalls you' with پیش 'before/in front of'; هولناک is a postpositive adjective 'terrifying' describing the cave. The order is compressed and poetic: 'a certain terrifying cave comes before you.'
segments.s116-g60: چنان چون شنیدم is an evidential/echo phrase 'such as I heard' or 'as I heard it' (چنان = 'such', چون = 'as'), with شنیدم 'I heard' in the simple past. پر از بیم و باک is a standard adjective phrase 'full of fear and dread' (پر 'full (of)' + از + noun). The clause reports the speaker's report about the cave.
segments.s116-g61: گذارت is the deverbal noun with enclitic ت = 'your passage' or 'your going'; بران is a poetic contraction of بر آن 'upon/there' meaning 'thereon/there'. نره qualifies دیوان as 'male' or 'virile' (i.e. fierce) and جنگ names the action 'war/hostilities'. The clause describes what happens at your passage to that place.
segments.s116-g62: همه رزم را is the object phrase 'the whole combat/war' (همه 'whole' + رزم 'battle' + را object marker). ساخته is perfect/past participle 'made/arranged' (has made), and چون پلنگ is a simile 'like a leopard'. Together: 'they have made the entire fight like a leopard.'
segments.s116-g63: به غار اندرون 'into/within the cave' (به + غار + اندرون 'inside/within'), followed by گاه which here functions in a locative/poetic sense (not the common temporal sense) to mark the spot, then دیو سپید 'the white demon' (دیو = demon, سپید = white). The phrase locates the white demon inside the cave.
segments.s116-g64: کزویند is a fused/archaic form meaning 'from him/from which' (i.e. 'from whom/from which'), لشکر 'army' is the subject that issues forth, and به بیم و امید 'with fear and hope' is an adverbial phrase describing their emotional condition. The clause says the army comes forth from that demon with mixed fear and expectation.
segments.s116-g65: توانی is a second‑person modal ('can you'); مگر here softens the question ('perhaps / can you possibly'); کردن ... تباه is an infinitival construction where کردن (to make/cause) takes the object او را and the adjective تباه as result — literally 'to cause him ruined' = 'to destroy him'. را marks او as the definite object.
segments.s116-g66: که introduces an explanatory/casual clause ('for / since / that'); اویست is the fused form of او + است ('he is') in poetic spelling; سالار is a predicate noun 'commander, chief'; و links the two predicates and پشت سپاه is a nominal phrase meaning 'the support/backbone of the army' (پشت here = support/rear).
segments.s116-g67: سپه را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker ('as for the army'); ز غم indicates cause ('from sorrow'); چشمها is the plural subject 'the eyes'; تیره شد is a past construction ('became dark/dim') where شد is the past of شدن used as copula/auxiliary.
segments.s116-g68: مرا here functions as a dative/possessive ('to me' → 'my') in classical poetic usage (equivalent to 'my' before چشم); در تیرگی is a locative phrase 'in the darkness/gloom'; خیره شد is past 'became fixed/stared' (شد as auxiliary). Word order (dative + noun + locative + verb) is typical of older/persian poetic syntax.
segments.s116-g69: پزشکان is the plural subject 'the physicians'; به درمانش is a prepositional phrase 'for his cure' (درمان + pronominal suffix -ش); کردند امید is an inverted idiomatic order meaning 'they made/placed hope' → 'they hoped'. کردند is past plural of کردن.
segments.s116-g70: به introduces the means/instrument ('by/with'); خون دل is a genitive construction 'blood of the heart' (heart's blood); و links the two remedies and مغز دیو سپید means 'the brain of the white demon' (noun + adjective order is normal in classical Persian: دیو سپید = 'white demon').
segments.s116-g71: چنین 'thus' + گفت (past) 'said/spoke' begins the reported speech; فرزانه مردی پزشک is an appositive phrase: 'a wise man, a physician' — مردی here is indefinite ('a certain man') and پزشک identifies his profession. Word order places the speaker description after the verb, which is normal in narrative.
segments.s116-g72: که introduces the reported clause ('that' / 'when'); چون is used here as 'when / as'; خون او را is 'his blood' with را marking the noun phrase; بسان is an archaic poetic simile meaning 'like / as'; سرشک is a diminutive 'teardrop' (a small drop), completing the simile 'his blood like a teardrop.'
segments.s116-g49: که introduces a subordinate clause here meaning 'when/that'. نزدیک ... شد is a predicate: نزدیک (approaching/near) with شد (past of شدن) gives 'became near / drew near'; کاووس is the goal ('to Kavus'). The subject پیلتن comes after شد (classical inversion), so the sequence reads 'that Pīltan became/was near Kavus'.
segments.s116-g50: همه quantifies the following noun phrase (all). نره modifies دیوان as 'male' (i.e. 'male divs' or 'male members'); شوند (3rd pl. subjunctive/present of شدن) is the main verb meaning 'become/gather (together)'; انجمن is the predicate noun 'assembly/meeting' — together: 'all the male divs became an assembly/gathered'.
segments.s116-g51: همه رنجهای تو is the subject 'all your sufferings' (رنجهای = plural of رنج, followed by the possessive pronoun تو). بی‌بر is a predicate adjective meaning 'fruitless / without fruit' placed before the verb; شود (3rd sing. of شدن) yields 'become / come to be' — so 'all your sufferings will become fruitless'.
segments.s116-g52: ز is the preposition 'from / because of' introducing the cause (here the divs). جهان is the theme 'the world', followed by the adjective phrase پر ز لشکر 'full of hosts' (پر = 'full (of)'; the second ز marks the complement 'of armies'). شود is the verb 'become' — overall: 'because of the divs, the world becomes full of armies'.
segments.s116-g53: تو اکنون are topicalized pronoun and time adverb 'you now'. ره (راه) means 'the way / road' and خانه دیو is the destination 'the demon's house' (noun + genitive). گیر is an imperative of گرفتن ('take' / 'go by'), here used without an initial ب- (a common classical/poetic imperative form), so it issues the command 'take the road to the demon's house'.
segments.s116-g54: به رنج introduces the circumstance 'into hardship / to hardship'. اندرآور is a causative/imperative verb 'bring/put into' (addressing the same addressee), so together 'put/bring into hardship'. تن و تیغ و تیر is a coordinated list 'body and sword and arrow' as the objects of that verb; Persian lists use و between items.
segments.s116-g55: مگر here functions as a conditional/introductory particle (classical/poetic 'if / if only / provided that'); یار 'ally' is the predicate noun; باشدت is a classical subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may be / be to you' with the enclitic -ت marking the 2nd person — together 'if the pure God be your ally'. یزدان پاک is the divine name 'the pure God' modifying یار.
segments.s116-g56: سر جادوان is a genitive phrase 'the heads of the magicians' (سر = head, جادوان = magicians, plural). اندر آری is the verb phrase 'put/bring into' or 'lay into' — آری here is an archaic verbal form ('he/you will bring') derived from آوردن (not represented among the provided senses), and اندر marks 'into/among'. به خاک 'to the dust/ground' is the goal phrase. The clause as a whole says that those heads will be put to the ground/dusted.
segments.s116-g9: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. رستم is the subject. بدیدش is past tense 'saw' with the enclitic -ش = 'him' attached to the verb (object pronoun). برانگیخت is a past-tense transitive verb 'he roused/spurred' and اسپ (steed) is its object; classical/poetic order allows the object after the verb here.
segments.s116-g10: بیامد is past 'came' (subject omitted; continues Rostam as actor). بر وی = 'against/upon him' (بر + pronoun وی gives direction toward the opponent). چو introduces a simile 'like', followed by the name آذر گشسپ (a compound/name) meaning 'Azar Goshasp'. The whole clause means he rode up to the foe like that famous figure.
segments.s116-g11: سر و گوش are coordinated direct objects ('head and ears') placed before the verb بگرفت (past 'seized'); Persian allows lists of objects before the verb in poetry. The second و introduces another object phrase یالش (yāl + enclitic -ش = 'its mane') modified by the adjective دلیر 'brave'. The verb بگرفت governs all these objects.
segments.s116-g12: سر از تن بکندش is literal 'he tore the head from the body of it': سر (head) + از (from) + تن (body) followed by بکندش (بکند + enclitic -ش) where the enclitic refers back to the head/body and the verb is past 'pulled off/tore off'. به کردار شیر is a prepositional simile 'in the manner of a lion' (به + کردار = 'in/by the mode of'), so the action is likened to a lion's behavior.
segments.s116-g13: پر از خون is a preposed descriptive phrase 'full of blood' (پر + از + خون) modifying the following noun. سر دیو is a genitive 'the demon's head' (noun + possessor). کنده is a past participle 'torn/detached' and ز تن ('from the body') completes that participial phrase; this compact sequence stacks modifiers before the main verb in typical epic diction.
segments.s116-g14: بینداخت is past 'he threw/cast' (subject again implied). ز آنسو here indicates direction toward 'that side' (آنسو) — in classical usage ز can mark spatial relation. که بود انجمن is a relative clause 'where/which there was an assembly' (که = 'that/where', بود = 'was', انجمن = 'assembly').
segments.s116-g15: چو marks a temporal clause 'when'. دیوان is the plural subject 'the demons' (here the sense list did not include the 'demon' meaning, so flagged below). بدیدند is past plural 'they saw'. گوپال is the proper name (Gupāl/Gopala) and the enclitic اوی after it functions as an emphatic/resumptive pronoun 'he/him' in classical/poetic usage.
segments.s116-g16: بدریدشان is past 'cut/tore' with the enclitic -شان referring to 'them' (the demons): the verb + clitic yields 'he tore (for) them' → 'he tore their ...'. دل is the direct object 'hearts'. ز introduces the ablative/instrumental phrase 'from/with' and چنگال اوی = 'his claws' (چنگال 'claw' + اوی possessive 'his'), so the clause says his claws tore out their hearts.

sentences.s116-s17: A roar arose and reached the ear; my spirit and heart were refreshed by that cry.
sentences.s116-s18: In Qobad’s time he did not make such a outcry; where did he fight a battle with the Turkish king?
sentences.s116-s19: At that same time he also came before him — a heroic, knowledge-illumining, quarrelsome fighter.
sentences.s116-s20: Piltan came near to Kavus; all the proud/victorious men gathered in council.
sentences.s116-s13: Tahmatan asked the young men for the road to the city where King Kavus was.
sentences.s116-s14: When he heard it, he quickly set his face and ran on foot forward toward him, the way-seeker.
sentences.s116-s15: When he entered the inner city, Tajbakhsh raised a cry like the thunder of Rakhsh.
sentences.s116-s16: Then he said to the Iranians, 'O sovereign, ill times have come upon us.'
sentences.s116-s1: A certain royal maghfarī (a pardoning/royal cap) was upon his head; "Take away his tainted habit and put it into it."
sentences.s116-s2: He turned his face toward Arzhang, the commander, when he came up to the fame‑seeking host.
sentences.s116-s3: Someone cried aloud among the company: 'You said you would tear apart sea and mountain.'
sentences.s116-s4: Out of the tent came Arzhang the demon, when that roar reached his ear.
sentences.s116-s21: They cried out loudly, offered prayers, and asked him about his long sufferings.
sentences.s116-s22: They took him into the embrace of King Kavus; they asked Zal about the hardships of the road.
sentences.s116-s23: He said to him, 'Keep him hidden from these sorcerers; you must make Rakhsh ready to go.'
sentences.s116-s24: When news came to the White Demon that Arzhang had made the face of the world empty.
sentences.s116-s37: If three drops fall into the inner eye, the clouding will be cleared and blood will come out.
sentences.s116-s38: Gupiltan made ready for war; from that place he began to march.
sentences.s116-s39: He told the Iranians, 'Be awake, be alert, for I have set my intent against the White Demon.'
sentences.s116-s40: One war‑elephant is a resource and, plentifully, there is an army in his retinue.
sentences.s116-s41: If now my back is brought to a bend, may you remain long despised and sullen.
sentences.s116-s42: But if there is an ally, may God grant me an auspicious, powerful star.
sentences.s116-s43: Recover that same homeland and throne, and that royal tree will come to bearing.
sentences.s116-s9: They did not remember their homeland and fled; father and son together set out on the same road.
sentences.s116-s10: The sword of Piltan’s vengeance was raised; he struck at once from that assembly.
sentences.s116-s11: When the victorious, world‑illuminating one returned, he came to the foot of Mount Asporoz.
sentences.s116-s12: From the offspring they opened the loop of the lasso; they sat down beneath a tall tree.
sentences.s116-s29: One must pass over seven mountains; bands of divs have formed in every place.
sentences.s116-s30: A certain cave, terrifying, appears before you; as I heard, it is full of fear and dread.
sentences.s116-s31: When you pass there, the male divs set about war; they have made the whole combat like a leopard.
sentences.s116-s32: Within the cave dwells a white demon; from him the army issues forth with fear and hope.
sentences.s116-s33: Can you possibly bring him to ruin? For he is the commander and the army's main support.
sentences.s116-s34: The army's eyes grew dim from sorrow; my gaze became fixed in the gloom.
sentences.s116-s35: The physicians put their hope in curing him — by (using) the heart's blood and the brain of the white demon.
sentences.s116-s36: Thus spoke a wise man, a physician: 'that when his blood was like a teardrop...'
sentences.s116-s25: When Pīltan drew near to Kavus, all the male divs gathered together into an assembly.
sentences.s116-s26: All your sufferings will come to nothing; because of the divs the world will be filled with hosts (armies).
sentences.s116-s27: You now take the road to the demon’s house; put your body, sword, and arrow into hardship.
sentences.s116-s28: If the pure God is your ally, you will lay the magicians' heads in the dust.
sentences.s116-s5: When Rostam saw him, he spurred his steed and, like Azar Goshasp, rode up to him.
sentences.s116-s6: He seized its head and ears and its brave mane, and, like a lion, tore the head from the body.
sentences.s116-s7: He cast the demon's head — torn from the body and full of blood — toward the side where the assembly was.
sentences.s116-s8: When the demons saw him — Gupāl — his claws tore out their hearts.
